title: BG Objection Practice Lab
disclaimer: These scenarios and coaching prompts are Dyer practice guidance. They are not transcripts or verbatim BG scripts.

scenarios.title: Can't Afford It
scenarios.vehicle: 2019 midsize SUV · 72,400 miles · no coolant exchange history
scenarios.customerContext: The customer approved the safety work but says the remaining maintenance is outside today's budget.
scenarios.customerQuote: I understand why you're recommending it, but I can't afford anything else today.
scenarios.practiceGoal: Protect the relationship, learn the actual constraint, and offer a practical next step without discount pressure.

framework.acknowledge: Recognize that the customer has already made a spending decision today.
framework.clarify: Ask whether the concern is the total amount today or the priority of this service.
framework.connect: Restate the coolant finding and the system it helps protect, without predicting a failure.
framework.ask: Offer to document it and agree on a specific revisit point if today is not workable.

scenarios.coachFor: Calm prioritization and a clear follow-up path.
scenarios.avoid: Do not shame the customer, invent urgency, or promise that the service prevents every repair.

scenarios.title: Does It Really Work?
scenarios.vehicle: 2021 direct-injection crossover · 46,800 miles · reduced fuel economy noted at write-up
scenarios.customerContext: The technician documented a maintenance opportunity, but the customer is skeptical of chemical services.
scenarios.customerQuote: Does that treatment really work, or is it just something dealerships sell?
scenarios.practiceGoal: Answer the skepticism directly with the vehicle-specific reason for the recommendation.

framework.acknowledge: Treat the question as reasonable rather than defensive.
framework.clarify: Ask whether the customer wants to understand the procedure, the expected benefit, or both.
framework.connect: Tie the service to the engine design, the mileage, and the customer's fuel-economy concern.
framework.ask: Confirm whether the explanation gives enough information to make a decision.

scenarios.coachFor: Evidence-first language that explains purpose without exaggerated performance claims.
scenarios.avoid: Do not guarantee a mileage increase or describe the service as a cure-all.

scenarios.title: Don't Have Time
scenarios.vehicle: 2020 sedan · 54,100 miles · front brake work already approved · fluid test indicates contamination
scenarios.customerContext: The customer has a hard pickup deadline and assumes the added service will keep the vehicle all day.
scenarios.customerQuote: I don't have time for another service. I need the car back by four.
scenarios.practiceGoal: Separate the schedule concern from the value decision and give an honest timing answer.

framework.acknowledge: Repeat the pickup commitment so the customer knows it was heard.
framework.clarify: Confirm the firm deadline and check the actual shop time before answering.
framework.connect: Explain why the fluid recommendation fits the approved brake work and current test result.
framework.ask: Offer the realistic options: complete it within the confirmed window or schedule it for a defined return visit.

scenarios.coachFor: Accurate timing, no guessing, and a clean reschedule path.
scenarios.avoid: Do not say it will only take a few minutes unless the shop has confirmed that.

scenarios.title: Need to Talk to My Spouse
scenarios.vehicle: 2018 pickup · 68,900 miles · no transmission service in available history
scenarios.customerContext: Both spouses make maintenance decisions, and the person on the phone wants clear information to share.
scenarios.customerQuote: I need to talk to my spouse before I approve that.
scenarios.practiceGoal: Respect shared decision-making and make the recommendation easy to relay accurately.

framework.acknowledge: Agree that a shared decision is reasonable.
framework.clarify: Ask what information would help the customer explain the recommendation at home.
framework.connect: Summarize the vehicle history, why fluid condition matters, and what the service does in plain language.
framework.ask: Offer a text or written estimate and set a no-pressure follow-up time.

scenarios.coachFor: A concise, shareable explanation and permission-based follow-up.
scenarios.avoid: Do not try to route around the spouse or create a false deadline.

scenarios.title: Is That Under Warranty?
scenarios.vehicle: 2023 compact SUV · 31,600 miles · factory powertrain coverage may still apply
scenarios.customerContext: The customer is mixing up repair coverage, routine maintenance, and BG protection-plan eligibility.
scenarios.customerQuote: If this is important, shouldn't it be covered under my warranty?
scenarios.practiceGoal: Distinguish preventive maintenance from a covered failure and avoid promising any protection coverage.

framework.acknowledge: Confirm that checking coverage before spending money is smart.
framework.clarify: Ask whether the customer means the factory warranty, a service contract, or BG protection coverage.
framework.connect: Explain that today's item is preventive maintenance, not a diagnosed failed component.
framework.ask: Offer to verify the applicable coverage and eligibility before making any promise.

scenarios.coachFor: Precise use of the words maintenance, repair, warranty, and eligibility.
scenarios.avoid: Do not claim factory or BG coverage without confirming the current program and vehicle record.

scenarios.title: Never Had to Do That Before
scenarios.vehicle: 2020 turbocharged direct-injection sedan · 58,200 miles · first visit to Dyer
scenarios.customerContext: The customer's previous vehicle and previous shop never presented this service.
scenarios.customerQuote: I've owned cars for thirty years and I've never had to do that before.
scenarios.practiceGoal: Honor the customer's experience while returning the conversation to this vehicle and this finding.

framework.acknowledge: Recognize the customer's long ownership experience.
framework.clarify: Ask about previous maintenance history and whether a similar service may have had a different name.
framework.connect: Explain the relevant engine design, mileage, symptoms, or inspection evidence on this vehicle.
framework.ask: Invite the customer to decide based on today's facts, not a blanket rule.

scenarios.coachFor: Respectful contrast between past experience and current evidence.
scenarios.avoid: Do not imply older advice was wrong or that every newer vehicle needs the same service.

scenarios.title: Not Having Any Issues
scenarios.vehicle: 2019 AWD crossover · 64,700 miles · original driveline fluid in available history
scenarios.customerContext: The vehicle drives normally, so the customer sees no reason to maintain the fluid.
scenarios.customerQuote: It's running fine. I'm not having any issues with it.
scenarios.practiceGoal: Explain preventive timing without using fear or suggesting a problem that was not diagnosed.

framework.acknowledge: Confirm that there is no current symptom or diagnosed failure.
framework.clarify: Ask whether the customer prefers maintenance before symptoms or only after a problem appears.
framework.connect: Explain that the recommendation is based on fluid service history and protecting a working system.
framework.ask: Present the choice clearly and document a follow-up interval if declined.

scenarios.coachFor: A clean distinction between preventive service and repair.
scenarios.avoid: Do not turn normal operation into a hidden-failure story.

scenarios.title: Do I Really Need It?
scenarios.vehicle: 2017 SUV · 83,300 miles · dark hydraulic steering fluid documented
scenarios.customerContext: The customer wants a direct answer about urgency and priority.
scenarios.customerQuote: Be honest with me. Do I really need this today?
scenarios.practiceGoal: Give an honest priority statement and let the documented condition carry the recommendation.

framework.acknowledge: Thank the customer for asking for a straight answer.
framework.clarify: Separate a safety-critical repair from a preventive maintenance recommendation.
framework.connect: Reference the observed fluid condition and the components the fluid supports.
framework.ask: Recommend a timing window and confirm the customer's decision without pressure.

scenarios.coachFor: Direct priority language: required now, recommended now, or plan soon.
scenarios.avoid: Do not label optional maintenance as required.

scenarios.title: It's Not in the Owner's Manual
scenarios.vehicle: 2022 minivan · 42,500 miles · musty odor reported when A/C starts
scenarios.customerContext: The customer checked the maintenance schedule and did not find the exact branded service name.
scenarios.customerQuote: I looked in the owner's manual and it doesn't say I need this.
scenarios.practiceGoal: Agree with what the manual does and does not say, then explain the symptom-based recommendation.

framework.acknowledge: Confirm that the customer is right to consult the manufacturer schedule.
framework.clarify: Ask whether the concern is schedule compliance or the odor reported today.
framework.connect: Explain that this recommendation responds to the documented cabin-air concern, not a claim that the manufacturer requires BG branding.
framework.ask: Offer the service as an option and explain what outcome the shop is trying to address.

scenarios.coachFor: Honest manufacturer-language boundaries and symptom-based value.
scenarios.avoid: Never say the manufacturer requires a branded service unless verified in current manufacturer information.

scenarios.title: Need to Think About It
scenarios.vehicle: 2021 crossover · 49,000 miles · fluid test result recorded on MPI
scenarios.customerContext: The customer is not saying no, but needs a pause before deciding.
scenarios.customerQuote: Let me think about that before I add anything else.
scenarios.practiceGoal: Give the customer space while making sure the decision is informed and follow-up is specific.

framework.acknowledge: Accept the pause without immediately repeating the pitch.
framework.clarify: Ask whether a missing fact, cost, timing, or priority is causing uncertainty.
framework.connect: Restate the single strongest reason for the recommendation and then stop talking.
framework.ask: Agree on when the customer wants to decide and how the advisor should follow up.

scenarios.coachFor: One useful clarification, one concise recap, and no pressure loop.
scenarios.avoid: Do not treat 'think about it' as permission to restart the entire presentation.

scenarios.title: Does the Manufacturer Require It?
scenarios.vehicle: 2020 pickup · 59,600 miles · severe-use towing history documented
scenarios.customerContext: The customer wants to know whether the recommendation is a manufacturer requirement or dealership advice.
scenarios.customerQuote: Does the manufacturer require this, or is this just your recommendation?
scenarios.practiceGoal: Answer the exact question and distinguish schedule information from condition- and use-based advice.

framework.acknowledge: Say it is a fair distinction.
framework.clarify: Check the current manufacturer schedule and the vehicle's actual use before stating a requirement.
framework.connect: Explain whether the recommendation comes from the schedule, towing use, fluid condition, service history, or a combination.
framework.ask: Offer to show the documented basis and let the customer choose.

scenarios.coachFor: Source accuracy and a clear line between required and recommended.
scenarios.avoid: Do not invent a manufacturer interval or imply BG branding is manufacturer-required.

scenarios.title: Sounds Expensive
scenarios.vehicle: 2018 luxury SUV · 76,100 miles · multiple maintenance items due for review
scenarios.customerContext: The customer reacted to the package name before hearing the actual price or included work.
scenarios.practiceGoal: Replace package jargon with a clear scope, price, and priority explanation.

framework.acknowledge: Agree that the name alone does not explain the value.
framework.clarify: Ask whether the concern is the total price or uncertainty about what is included.
framework.connect: Break down the included services, the trigger for each, and which items can be prioritized separately.
framework.ask: Present the exact price and ask which option best fits the customer's plan.

scenarios.coachFor: Transparent scope and price without defensive value stacking.
scenarios.avoid: Do not compare maintenance cost to an unverified worst-case repair bill.

scenarios.title: Not Interested
scenarios.vehicle: 2022 sedan · 38,700 miles · no urgent repair; maintenance recommendation only
scenarios.customerContext: The customer gives a firm, brief decline and appears ready to end the conversation.
scenarios.customerQuote: No, I'm not interested in that.
scenarios.practiceGoal: Respect a clear decline, preserve trust, and document the recommendation correctly.

framework.clarify: Only if tone allows, ask one permission-based question to identify whether cost, timing, or value was the concern.
framework.connect: If permission is not given, do not repeat the recommendation; document the original trigger.
framework.ask: Confirm what is approved today and whether the customer wants the item revisited later.

scenarios.coachFor: Knowing when to stop and keeping the approved work moving.
scenarios.avoid: Do not challenge a firm no or hide the decline from the RO.

scenarios.title: No Thanks
scenarios.vehicle: 2019 sedan · 67,200 miles · coolant service due for history review
scenarios.customerContext: The customer declines politely before hearing the inspection evidence.
scenarios.customerQuote: No thanks. Just do what I came in for.
scenarios.practiceGoal: Honor the boundary while checking whether the customer wants one factual sentence for the record.

framework.acknowledge: Confirm the original visit work will stay on track.
framework.clarify: Ask permission to share the single inspection fact that prompted the recommendation.
framework.connect: If permission is granted, explain the finding briefly; if not, stop.
framework.ask: Confirm the approved work and document a respectful follow-up note.

scenarios.coachFor: Permission-based communication after an early decline.
scenarios.avoid: Do not treat politeness as uncertainty that needs to be overcome.

scenarios.title: It's a Lease
scenarios.vehicle: 2023 leased SUV · 34,800 miles · return planned in about nine months · fluid test result documented
scenarios.customerContext: The customer believes any maintenance that outlasts the lease has no personal value.
scenarios.customerQuote: No thanks, it's a lease. I won't own it long enough for that to matter.
scenarios.practiceGoal: Use the remaining term, current condition, and lease obligations rather than long-term ownership language.

framework.acknowledge: Agree that the remaining ownership horizon changes the decision.
framework.clarify: Ask about the planned return date and whether the customer has lease-maintenance requirements to verify.
framework.connect: Explain only the current operational or documented maintenance value that applies during the remaining term.
framework.ask: Offer to verify requirements and let the customer decide based on the actual remaining use.

scenarios.coachFor: Customer-specific value instead of lifetime-ownership assumptions.
scenarios.avoid: Do not claim the lease requires the service without checking the customer's agreement and current manufacturer information.
